2017 · SemEval-2017 · Benchmarks & Evals

SemEval-2017 Task 1: Semantic Textual Similarity Multilingual and Crosslingual Focused Evaluation

The 2017 multilingual/cross-lingual STS shared task that also introduced the STS Benchmark — a fixed, 8,628-pair, 0–5 human-scored English dataset that nearly every sentence-embedding paper since (InferSent, SentEval, Sentence-BERT, SimCSE) reports a number against.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Daniel Cer, Mona Diab, Eneko Agirre, Iñigo Lopez-Gazpio, Lucia Specia
venue SemEval-2017 citations 2,131 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) — scoring how close two sentences are in meaning on a graded scale — had run as a SemEval/*SEM shared task every year since 2012, but by 2017 the series had two problems. First, it was overwhelmingly English and monolingual: 2012–2013 were English-only, 2014–2015 added a Spanish track, and 2016 added a small pilot Spanish-English cross-lingual track, but Arabic, Turkish, and genuinely cross-lingual pairings spanning four languages had never been evaluated together in one task. Second — and more consequential for the field long-term — five years of STS had produced five years of differently-sized, differently-sourced data sets (MSRpar, MSRvid, OnWN, news headlines, forum posts, image captions, and more) with no fixed train/dev/test split and no single citable snapshot. Papers building sentence embeddings evaluated on their own cherry-picked subset of years, which made cross-paper comparison unreliable just as the sentence-embedding literature (deep averaging networks, LSTM encoders, weighted word-embedding baselines) was starting to take off.

SemEval-2017 Task 1 addresses both gaps at once. It restructures the shared task around Arabic, Spanish, English and Turkish — monolingual and cross-lingual, plus a dedicated machine-translation quality-estimation sub-track — and, in the part with the longer half-life, releases the STS Benchmark: a fixed, partitioned selection of the 2012–2017 English STS data that the field could finally cite by name instead of re-deriving its own ad hoc slice every time.

// how it works

The method

The task itself is simple: annotators score a pair of sentences on the ordinal 0–5 scale from Table 1 (0 = completely dissimilar, 5 = meaning-equivalent, with interpretable half-steps in between), and systems are ranked by Pearson correlation (r) between their predicted scores and the gold human labels. Six tracks span four languages: track 1 Arabic (ar-ar), track 2 Arabic-English (ar-en), track 3 Spanish (es-es), track 4a Spanish-English drawn from SNLI, track 4b Spanish-English drawn from WMT machine-translation quality-estimation data, track 5 English (en-en), and track 6 Turkish-English — run as a genuine "surprise language" track, with the language pair's identity withheld until evaluation data was released. Each track contributes 250 pairs for 1,750 evaluation pairs total. The primary ranking averages correlation across tracks 1–5 (with 4a and 4b scored individually); Turkish-English is excluded from the primary score because of its surprise-language status.

Most pairs derive from the Stanford Natural Language Inference (SNLI) corpus (Flickr30k image captions). Rather than reusing SNLI's original entailment pairings — which would let the entailment label leak information into the STS score — the authors construct fresh pairings using a cosine-similarity heuristic over summed word embeddings, then send only the newly-formed pairs for annotation. Non-English versions are produced by human translation: Arabic via CMU-Qatar post-editing of machine translation, Spanish by a single native-speaker graduate student, Turkish via SDL, with the English STS label carried over to the translated pair. Track 4b instead pulls directly from WMT14 quality-estimation data — English source sentences and their machine translations, independently annotated for STS by a single expert rather than by crowd workers, because judging the subtle meaning drift introduced by MT errors is a harder, more specialized task than judging two independently-written sentences.

Annotation for every other track is crowdsourced on Amazon Mechanical Turk: five ratings per pair from Master-qualified workers, paid $1 per 20-pair batch, with the gold score set to the mean of the five. The paper reports no inter-annotator agreement or reliability statistic for this process anywhere in the text — a point picked up in the critique below.

Finally, the paper packages the English-only slice of 2012–2017 STS data into the STS Benchmark: 8,628 sentence pairs split 5,749 train / 1,500 dev / 1,379 test across three genres (news, image/video captions, forum posts), explicitly intended as a standing evaluation set rather than a one-off shared-task snapshot — the artifact now universally known as "STS-B."

simv(s₁, s₂) = v(s₁) · v(s₂)‖v(s₁)‖ ‖v(s₂)‖ // cosine similarity over summed 50-d GloVe word vectors, v(s) = Σw∈s v(w) — used only to surface non-trivial SNLI sentence pairs for human annotation. Systems themselves are scored by Pearson r against the resulting 0–5 gold labels, not by this heuristic.
// what they found

The results

The task drew strong participation: 31 teams producing 84 submissions, with 17 teams contributing 44 systems that covered every track. ECNU is best overall (average Pearson r = 0.7316 across tracks 1–5), an ensemble of three feature-engineered regressors (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost over n-gram overlap, edit distance, tree kernels, word alignments, and MT-evaluation-metric features) with four deep sentence-embedding models (averaged, projected, DAN, and LSTM encoders). ECNU also tops track 2 Arabic-English (r = 0.7493), track 3 Spanish (r = 0.8559), and track 6 English-Turkish (r = 0.7706). BIT takes track 1 Arabic (r = 0.7543); CompiLIG wins track 4a Spanish-English/SNLI (r = 0.8302); RTV edges out DT_Team on track 5 English (r = 0.8547 vs. 0.8536); and SEF@UHH's paragraph-vector model is best on the hard track 4b MT quality-estimation pairs, but only at r = 0.3407 — by far the weakest winning score of any track. The simple cosine-of-binary-sentence-vectors baseline (with Google-Translate MT for cross-lingual pairs) reaches an average r of 53.70 across tracks 1–5, which would place it 23rd of the 44 systems that ran on every track.

Digging into why track 4b is so much harder: the paper reports that gold MT-quality scores correlate with gold STS scores at only r = 0.41 — evidence that "how good is this translation" and "how similar in meaning are these two sentences" are related but genuinely different signals, even though MT quality-estimation pairs are translations of the same source sentence. Meanwhile, on Track 5 English, roughly 80% of top systems' scores land within 1.0 point of the gold label once rescaled to 0–5 — reasonable agreement in aggregate, but Table 12's hand-picked hard cases (below) show exactly where the remaining 20% comes from.

TrackBest systemPearson r×100
1 — Arabic (ar-ar)BIT75.43
2 — Arabic-English (ar-en)ECNU74.93
3 — Spanish (es-es)ECNU85.59
4a — Spanish-English, SNLI (es-en)CompiLIG83.02
4b — Spanish-English, MT quality est. (es-en)SEF@UHH34.07
5 — English (en-en)RTV85.47
6 — English-Turkish, surprise track (tr-en)ECNU77.06
cosine baseline, avg. tracks 1–5—53.70

On the newly-introduced STS Benchmark test split (1,379 English pairs), the 2017 shared-task systems are compared against the sentence-embedding baselines that were emerging in the wider literature at the time. ECNU tops the benchmark at 81.0, followed by BIT (80.9), DT_TEAM (79.2), UdL (79.0) and HCTI (78.4). Among "sentence-level" baselines — pretrained, not tuned on STS — InferSent scores 75.8, Sent2Vec 75.5, SIF 72.0, PV-DBOW 64.9, and C-PHRASE 63.9. The plainest averaged-word-embedding baselines trail further behind: Word2vec 56.5, LexVec 55.8, FastText 53.9, Paragram 50.1, and GloVe 40.6. This single table — one fixed test split, one metric, every method scored the same way — is exactly the artifact the field was missing, and it is why STS-B rather than any single year's shared-task data became the number every subsequent sentence-embedding paper reports.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

STS-B became the field's default sentence-embedding yardstick almost by accident of timing: it landed in mid-2017, just as InferSent, SIF, Sent2Vec and (soon after) BERT-derived encoders needed a common, citable target, and it has been reported against essentially unchanged ever since. That longevity is worth scrutinizing on its own terms, using evidence already sitting inside this paper.

Start with what a single Pearson r number hides. Table 12 is the paper's own hard-case audit, and it is damning in a specific way: on "A man is carrying a canoe with a dog" vs. "A dog is carrying a man in a canoe" — same content words, reversed argument structure, human score 1.8 — every one of the five top English systems scores it 3.2–5.0, i.e. as substantially or completely similar. On the negation pair (goggles/swimming-cap "with" vs. "without"), scores range from 0.1 to 4.8 against a human score of 3.0. These are not edge cases invented for the critique — they are the paper's own selected illustrations of what a 70–85% correlation on the aggregate leaderboard is quietly averaging over. A benchmark whose headline metric is a single correlation coefficient cannot distinguish "uniformly decent understanding" from "correct on the easy 80% of pairs, systematically blind to negation and word order on the rest," and STS-B's design gives no way to separate the two after the fact.

Second, the paper is explicit that a single similarity number does not transfer across downstream needs: gold MT-quality-estimation scores correlate with gold STS scores at only r = 0.41 on the same underlying sentence pairs (Track 4b). If STS and a closely related task like translation-quality judgment diverge that much on identical sentences, the implicit assumption behind "a model with a higher STS-B number is a better sentence encoder for my downstream task" deserves more scrutiny than it usually gets in papers that cite STS-B as their sole embedding-quality signal.

Third, there is a subtler distributional issue than outright leakage. STS-B's sentence pool is SNLI (Flickr30k captions), re-paired by the authors specifically to keep entailment gold labels from leaking into STS scores — a sensible mitigation the paper describes explicitly. But the underlying sentence surface distribution is still SNLI, and SNLI is itself a standard supervised training corpus for sentence encoders (InferSent is trained on SNLI's entailment objective by design). Removing label leakage does not remove distributional familiarity: an encoder trained on SNLI sentences, then evaluated on STS pairs built from SNLI sentences, is being tested partly on in-distribution text, which is a different and easier-to-miss risk than the entailment-leakage problem the authors did address.

baselines

Fair for 2017: the STS Benchmark table spans averaged-word-embedding baselines (GloVe, Word2vec, FastText, LexVec, Paragram) through sentence-level pretrained encoders (InferSent, Sent2Vec, SIF, PV-DBOW, C-PHRASE) to the shared-task systems themselves — a reasonably diverse, contemporary spread. The gap is only visible in hindsight: the paper obviously predates BERT/ELMo-family contextual encoders, so its own "state of the art" (ECNU at r=0.7316 overall, 81.0 on the STS-B test set) was superseded within about a year, which is normal but worth remembering when a later paper cites 2017-era numbers as if they were still competitive.

contamination

No score-leakage: the authors deliberately re-pair SNLI sentences so entailment labels can't leak into STS gold scores. But the sentence-surface pool is still SNLI, which several sentence-encoder training pipelines (InferSent chief among them) train on directly — a distributional-familiarity risk distinct from, and not addressed by, the entailment-leakage fix.

what's missed

Whether a model's STS-B correlation improved because it got better at meaning in general, or because it got better at the paper's own five named failure modes (word-sense disambiguation, attribute importance, compositional/argument-order meaning, negation, semantic blending). Aggregate Pearson r cannot tell these apart, and no error-category breakdown is reported for the benchmark's systems beyond the six illustrative examples in Table 12.

would raise confidence

(1) Any inter-annotator agreement or label-reliability statistic — none is reported anywhere in this paper, despite every ranking depending on the mean of five $1-per-20-pairs MTurk judgments. (2) A held-out or periodically-refreshed slice of STS-B test, given the same 1,379 pairs have been public and re-used for eight-plus years. (3) A per-category breakdown (negation, word order, WSD, …) alongside the single Pearson number, so gains on the leaderboard could be attributed to specific linguistic competence rather than aggregate curve-fitting.

// bottom line

The multilingual/cross-lingual shared task this paper was nominally about is the less-remembered half of its legacy; STS Benchmark is the part that stuck, precisely because it gave the field one fixed number to argue over. That is also its weakness: a single Pearson correlation on a fixed, never-refreshed, SNLI-derived test set has been asked to stand in for "does this model understand sentence meaning" for the better part of a decade, and the paper's own Table 12 and its 0.41 STS-vs-MT-quality correlation both show, in the authors' own data, how much a single number like that can miss.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. SemEval-2017 Task 1 folded five years of fragmented English STS data (2012–2017) into the STS Benchmark — 8,628 pairs split 5,749/1,500/1,379 train/dev/test — the fixed artifact now known simply as "STS-B" that InferSent, SentEval, Sentence-BERT, and SimCSE all still report a number against.
  2. Systems are ranked by Pearson correlation with 0–5 human similarity judgments; ECNU won overall (avg. r = 0.7316) by ensembling feature-engineered regressors with deep sentence encoders, but the paper's own hard-case table shows even the best systems badly mis-scoring negation and argument-order swaps that any single correlation number averages away.
  3. The multilingual/cross-lingual half of the task — Arabic, Spanish, Turkish, and an MT-quality-estimation sub-track — is the less-cited part of this paper today, but it's where the cracks show most: cross-lingual correlations trail English by 10+ points, the best MT quality-estimation system reaches only r = 0.34, and gold STS scores correlate with gold MT-quality scores at just r = 0.41 on the same sentence pairs — the paper's own evidence that one similarity number doesn't transfer cleanly across downstream needs.
